Abstract
--------

Computer-aided drug discovery has been around for decades, although the past few years have seen a tectonic shift towards embracing computational technologies in both academia and pharma. This shift is largely defined by the flood of data on ligand properties and binding to therapeutic targets and their 3D structures, abundant computing capacities and the advent of on-demand virtual libraries of drug-like small molecules in their billions. Taking full advantage of these resources requires fast computational methods for effective ligand screening. This includes structure-based virtual screening of gigascale chemical spaces, further facilitated by fast iterative screening approaches. Highly synergistic are developments in deep learning predictions of ligand properties and target activities in lieu of receptor structure. Here we review recent advances in ligand discovery technologies, their potential for reshaping the whole process of drug discovery and development, as well as the challenges they encounter. We also discuss how the rapid identification of highly diverse, potent, target-selective and drug-like ligands to protein targets can democratize the drug discovery process, presenting new opportunities for the cost-effective development of safer and more effective small-molecule treatments.

Abstract
--------

Computer-aided drug design (CADD), a cornerstone of modern drug discovery, can predict how a molecular structure relates to its activity and interacts with its target using structure-based and ligand-based methods. Fueled by ever-increasing data availability and continuous model optimization, artificial intelligence drug design (AIDD), as an enhanced iteration of CADD, has thrived in the past decade. AIDD demonstrates unprecedented opportunities in protein folding, property prediction, and molecular generation. It can also facilitate target identification, high-throughput screening (HTS), and synthetic route prediction. With AIDD involved, the process of drug discovery is greatly accelerated. Notably, AIDD offers the potential to explore uncharted territories of chemical space beyond current knowledge. In this perspective, we began by briefly outlining the main workflows and components of CADD. Then through showcasing exemplary cases driven by AIDD in recent years, we describe the evolving role of artificial intelligence (AI) in drug discovery from three distinct stages, that is, chemical library screening, linker generation, and _de novo_ molecular generation. In this process, we attempted to draw comparisons between the features of CADD and AIDD.

* * *

CADD and AIDD contribute to the drug discovery.[![Image 9: graphic file with name d4md00522h-ga.jpg](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/cb6f/11636611/c4a1a65973d9/d4md00522h-ga.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=11636611_d4md00522h-ga.jpg)

1. Introduction
---------------

The origins of CADD can be traced back to the pioneering work of Hansch _et al._ in the 1960s, who introduced the concept of quantitative structure–activity relationship (QSAR) modeling ([Fig. 1A](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#fig1)).[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit1) Emerging in the 1970s, the concept of CADD began to take shape.[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit2) By the 1980s, CADD had evolved into a robust tool in pharmaceutical research, utilizing computational power to streamline the entire workflow of drug discovery and development.[3–6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit3) It can perform a wide range of tasks, from identifying potential drug leads to optimizing their physicochemical and biological properties, thus contributing to a substantial reduction in both the financial cost and time required for drug discovery.[7,8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit7) CADD can mainly be divided into two categories, namely structure-based drug design (SBDD) and ligand-based drug design (LBDD), depending on whether the structure of the target is utilized.[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit9) Within SBDD strategies, molecular docking, molecular dynamics (MD), and structure-based virtual screening (SBVS) are frequently employed techniques.[10,11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit10) The discovery of zanamivir (published in 1994) and oseltamivir (published in 1997), used to treat influenza viruses, marked the beginning of SBDD.[12–14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit12) At a similar time, indinavir/L-735524 (published in 1994) as a potent HIV protease inhibitor was designed using molecular modeling and X-ray crystallography.[6,15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit6) In 1982, Kuntz _et al._ proposed the program Dock.[16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit16) Molecular docking is commonly used to identify potential biologically active compounds and analyze molecular recognition events. A year later, the software CHARMM was developed for MD simulations. MD simulations can be utilized to examine ligand–receptor interactions thoroughly. The combination of docking and MD simulations led to the discovery of amprenavir.[17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit17) Besides molecular docking and MD, additional methods such as free energy perturbation (FEP), thermodynamic integration (TI), and linear response (LR), as well as molecular mechanics Poisson–Boltzmann surface area (MM-PBSA), and molecular mechanics-generalized Born surface area (MM-GBSA) can be applied to enhance the prediction accuracy of the binding affinity or energy. Another primary component of CADD, LBDD, centers on the structure of the ligand itself and is applicable even when the receptor or its structure is unknown.[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit18) In LBDD, 3D QSAR and ligand-based pharmacophore modeling stand out as fundamental and widely used tools, providing essential insights into the interactions between ligands and their possible receptors. The beginning of LBDD was the approval of norfloxacin in 1986, which used the 2D-QSAR approach. Later, comparative molecular field analysis (CoMFA) based on 3D-QSAR was extensively employed. Both SBDD and LBDD methods are helpful tools. The combination of them has proven successful in exploring structural, chemical, and biological data.[11,19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit11) For example, the discovery of donepezil utilized both docking and 3D-QSAR techniques. However, with the exponential growth of data[20,21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit20) in recent years, alternative approaches, notably those capable of enhancing their performance through data-driven learning,[22,23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit22) have also garnered much attention.[24,25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit24)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/figure/fig1/)

AIDD is presented with unprecedented opportunities as an increasing amount of data on the biological activity and properties of chemical compounds becomes available. While the application of AI in drug discovery and development has become a trend in recent years ([Fig. 1B](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#fig1)), it is worth noting that the term “artificial intelligence” was formally introduced by John McCarthy as early as 1956.[26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit26) In concept, AI emulates human intelligence: by autonomously learning from input data or experiences, it can make complex decisions and solve novel problems.[25,27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit25) Its success extends across diverse applications, including computer games, speech recognition, computer vision (CV), and natural language processing (NLP).[28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit28) For instance, in the field of protein folding, AlphaFold2 can regularly achieve atomic accuracy even without structurally similar homologous proteins.[29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit29) As a significant paradigm within AI, machine learning (ML) utilizes algorithms to classify, regress, and cluster data by discerning its underlying patterns.[30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit30) Deep learning (DL), in turn, is a subset of ML. DL harnesses multi-layer neural networks to extract relationships within data. Neural networks consist of interconnected computing elements called “perceptrons”, inspired by the principle of biological neurons. However, their operation differs significantly from the human brain.[31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit31) Early ML models contain decision trees, random forest (RF), and support vector machines (SVMs), while DL algorithms primarily consist of deep neural networks, convolutional neural networks (CNNs), and recurrent neural networks (RNNs).[32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit32) In CADD, techniques utilize manually coded software programs to perform specific tasks. In contrast, AIDD techniques are trained on abundant data and algorithms, enabling them to learn and execute tasks autonomously.[33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit33) AI extracts and learns the information in the database.[34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit34) Data inputs and powerful algorithms are crucial to drug discovery with AIDD, corresponding to the two most critical parts of the process: database establishment and model training.[21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit21) With the improvement of computing power and optimization of models, this new method is expected to reduce labor and time costs more than CADD. Importantly, AIDD not only uses the model to search for candidate molecules in the known compound library but also fully explores the unknown molecular structure space by generating new molecular structures beyond existing experience and data.[35](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit35) The impact of AI on drug design is steadily expanding, with notable achievements in different aspects, such as property and activity prediction, _de novo_ design, and retrosynthetic approaches.

In this review, we elaborated on the changes in the field of drug discovery resulting from the technological shift from CADD to AIDD. We briefly outlined the main workflows and components of CADD and showcased successful cases of AIDD in recent years. To highlight the evolving role of AI in drug discovery, three distinct strategies for AIDD are elaborated, with different models at each stage: models predicting molecular properties, models linking known ligands, and models generating entirely new molecules. Finally, we made a summary and looked forward to the future of AIDD. It will play a key role in drug design in terms of property prediction, HTS, and linker and molecule generation.

2. Case study by CADD
---------------------

CADD was first conceptualized in the 1970s, and its applications in drug discovery have gradually increased since the 1980s.[36,37](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit36) Over decades, rooted in computational chemistry, CADD has evolved into a mature and promising methodology for discovering and optimizing drug leads. This method is applicable at different stages of drug discovery, enhancing the efficiency of the entire process.[38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit38) The efficiency brought by CADD not only accelerates new drug development but also mitigates costs.[17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit17)

CADD can be classified into two primary categories including SBDD and LBDD, depending on whether the structure of the target is employed ([Fig. 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#fig2)).[34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit34) Due to the necessity to exploit the structure of the target, SBDD incorporates two more steps than LBDD: the determination of the target structure and the identification of binding sites.[39](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit39) The structure of the target can typically be obtained from online repositories such as the Protein Data Bank (PDB).[40,41](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit40) These target structures are determined using various experimental techniques such as X-ray crystallography, nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, and cryo-electron microscopy (cryo-EM).[42–45](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit42) If the 3D structure of the target is not directly available in its online repository, computational methods like comparative modeling or homology modeling may be utilized to derive the 3D structure of the protein based on its amino acid sequences.[34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit34) As previously mentioned, AlphaFold represents a novel and valuable tool for predicting protein 3D structures. In addition, the identification of ligand binding sites on the target structure is also an essential step in SBDD.[46,47](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit46) Due to the dynamic nature of biomolecules and their propensity to undergo conformational shifts, accurately identifying ligand binding sites remains challenging.[48](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit48) Nevertheless, significant progress has been made in the past 20 years with the development and refinement of various computational methods, including both geometry-based and energy-based approaches, to streamline this process.[47,49–53](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit47) Recently, a few computational tools, such as Ligsite csc, Qsite finder, and CASTp, have demonstrated effective capabilities in identifying ligand binding sites.[10,54–56](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit10)

### Fig. 2. The main processes and sections of CADD.

Sequentially, molecular docking can be employed to investigate intermolecular interactions, thereby identifying the optimal binding conformation and predicting the binding affinity.[57–61](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit57) In the context of molecular docking, the two core methodologies are the searching function and the scoring function.[62](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit62) The former searches for all possible binding conformations to obtain the best spatial matching, while the latter evaluates the energy matching between the receptor and ligand to obtain conformations with lower binding energies.[63,64](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit63) Searching algorithms are expected to theoretically emulate experimental binding modes, and the acquired confirmations are to be assessed and ranked based on a scoring function.[65](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit65) Software programs and tools for molecular docking are available both commercially and free of charge, and they are widely used in both academia and industry.[66](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit66) Many early docking studies assume rigidity in both ligands and receptors, restricting all internal degrees of freedom of the ligand and receptor except for the three translational and rotational dimensions of the ligand.[66](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit66) To accelerate the performance of rigid docking algorithms, the advanced computation of physicochemical properties of receptors is conducted, and the results are stored at grid points established on the van der Waals surfaces of the receptor.[66–68](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit66) However, the rigidity approximation of ligand–receptor interactions lacks consideration for conformational changes, particularly when the cryptic site of an uncomplexed receptor opens under the influence of the ligand.[69](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit69) To explore additional potential binding conformations of the receptor, semi-flexible and flexible docking methods are specifically designed and employed.[70,71](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit70) Among the aforementioned docking methodologies, flexible docking requires the most computational power, as both receptor and ligand molecules undergo conformational changes.[72,73](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit72) The selection of the docking strategies depends on the characteristics of the system being studied as well as the specific purpose and context of the application.

Docking scores alone may be insufficient for ranking potential drug hits, as they often exhibit a weak correlation with experimental affinities. This observation suggests the need for alternative approaches to thoroughly study ligand–receptor interactions. Additional methods include MD, FEP, TI, LR, MM-PBSA, and MM-GBSA. Developed in the late 1970s, MD employs Newtonian force fields to predict the spatial coordinates of individual atoms within a molecular system over time, facilitating the computation of associated free energy.[74–76](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit74) The MD process comprises two main stages: the preparation of a computer model of the molecular system and the estimation of forces acting on each system atom.[77,78](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit77) Over the past few decades, many research efforts have been undertaken to accelerate the MD process and alleviate other limitations of the original methodology.[79](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit79) Notably, advanced force fields[80–84](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit80) and enhanced sampling techniques[79,85–93](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit79) have been proposed as promising solutions.

LBDD distinguishes itself by not mandating the utilization of receptor structures, providing flexibility for its application in scenarios where the target structure or even the target itself is either known or unknown. QSAR is an essential component of LBDD. QSAR involves the use of molecular descriptors and fingerprints, acquired by clustering molecules with similar structures to model the relationships between ligand structures and their biological activities.[42](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit42)

In CADD, two additional commonly used terms are pharmacophore modeling and virtual screening (VS). They both represent general strategies rather than specific methodologies. Pharmacophores demonstrate the most basic structural features of drug molecules, which facilitates the binding function of the molecules to their receptors. This approach can be categorized into structure-based pharmacophore modeling and ligand-based pharmacophore modeling.[94](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit94) The former indirectly derives the corresponding ligand pharmacophore characteristics from the receptor structure, while the latter directly utilizes the structural characteristics of the ligands. Ligand-based pharmacophore modeling commonly includes three steps: establishing the ligand training set, analyzing their conformations, and extracting their common chemical characteristics. These steps are undertaken to establish and verify the models.[34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit34) Besides, VS employs computer methods to sift through potential compounds in a large database. This can also be categorized into two main types: SBVS and ligand-based virtual screening (LBVS).[95](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit95) In contrast to the HTS strategy, VS does not necessitate direct biological experiments in the screening process, thereby significantly reducing the budget, time, and effort required.[96](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit96) It is worth noting that, in the past few years, many new VS methods have been proposed for screening ultra-large virtual libraries.[97–99](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit97) These new methods can screen significantly larger chemical libraries much faster than traditional VS methods, which require docking every compound in the libraries of interest into the receptor.

CADD is widely used in various stages of drug discovery. In [Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#tab1), we summarized some molecules successfully discovered with CADD during 2021–2023. In 2021, Stefan's team successfully identified inhibitors of a promiscuous ABC transporter through computer-aided pattern analysis.[100](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit100) Artem Cherkasov's group utilized screening to discover VPC-17281 and VPC-17160 (molecule 2 & 3) as potential treatments for prostate cancer by targeting androgen receptor (AR) and DNA binding domain (DBD) dimerization.[101](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit101) Additionally, in 2022, they utilized large-scale structure-based similarity search and scaffold tuning to identify VPC-70619 (molecule 6), which targets the N-Myc-Max DBD.[102](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit102) Chen and Cai's team discovered an inhibitor of the MyD88-Toll/interleukin-1 receptor domain, through screening a commercial library containing more than 40 000 compounds.[103](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit103) Hopkins's team utilized silico docking studies to discover compound 64 (molecule 5), an inhibitor of Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK).[104](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11523840/#cit104)

enriched_points.expanded_content: The efficacy of computational drug design relies heavily on high-quality datasets. Improved data harmonization across studies enhances prediction accuracy and model transferability.
Integrating expertise from computational biology, chemistry, and artificial intelligence fosters innovative drug discovery approaches, leveraging diverse methodologies for more effective solutions.

Adopting cutting-edge methods like deep learning and few-shot learning significantly accelerates drug discovery, facilitating rapid identification of potential drug candidates from vast chemical libraries.
